NJ School Data Almanac
Newark Public School District
ESSEX COUNTY  ·  TRADITIONAL PUBLIC DISTRICT  ·  DFG A  ·  43,216 STUDENTS (2026)

Ten things the data says about Newark Public School District. Every figure is computed from New Jersey Department of Education data via the njschooldata package — nothing is estimated. Comparisons use District Factor Group (DFG) peers (39 districts).

No. 1 ATTENDANCE

Newark’s Chronic Absenteeism Fell to 11.5%, Less Than Half the Rate Among Its Peer Districts

In 2024, 11.5% of Newark students were chronically absent, compared with a peer-group median of 24.1%.

New Jersey’s largest district has one of its lowest chronic absenteeism rates. In 2024, 11.5% of Newark Public School District students missed enough school to be flagged as chronically absent, less than half the median of 24.1% across the 39 districts in its District Factor Group A peer group.

The number is striking partly because of where Newark started. In 2019, before the pandemic disrupted school attendance everywhere, 26.8% of Newark students were chronically absent. The rate dipped to 16.6% in 2021, spiked back to 28.1% in 2022 as schools reopened, then fell sharply to 12.7% in 2023 and 11.5% in 2024.

That trajectory runs opposite to the national pattern, where chronic absence climbed and stuck after the pandemic. What did Newark change between 2022 and 2024 that cut its chronic-absence rate by more than half, and can the district hold the gain as enrollment keeps growing?

No. 2 GRADUATION

Newark’s Graduation Rate Hit 90% in 2025, Topping Four Out of Five Peer Districts

Newark’s four-year graduation rate reached 90.0% in 2025, an 81st-percentile result among 39 DFG A peers whose median was 85.0%.

Newark graduated 90.0% of its 2025 four-year cohort, a figure that places the district in the 81st percentile among the 39 districts in its District Factor Group A peer group, where the median rate is 85.0%.

The milestone caps a steady climb. The four-year rate sat at 80.8% in 2020 and 81.3% in 2021, then rose every year after: 85.1% in 2022, 85.7% in 2023, 86.3% in 2024, and 90.0% in 2025. The district crossed the 90% line even as the graduating cohort grew from 2,694 students in 2020 to 3,019 in 2025, meaning more students were graduating from a larger class rather than a shrinking one.

Nine-tenths of a cohort this size is roughly 2,700 diplomas in a single year. With the cohort still expanding alongside district enrollment, can Newark sustain a 90% rate as each graduating class gets larger?

No. 3 DEMOGRAPHICS

Hispanic Share of Newark Enrollment Rose From 43.8% to 62.1% Since 2015 as Black Share Fell 15.8 Points

Over eleven years Newark shifted from a roughly even split between Black and Hispanic students to a clear Hispanic majority.

In 2015, Newark’s student body was nearly evenly divided: 46.8% Black and 43.8% Hispanic. By 2026, the balance had tipped decisively. Students who are Hispanic now make up 62.1% of enrollment, an increase of 18.3 percentage points, while the share of students who are Black fell 15.8 points to 31.0%.

The shift was steady rather than sudden. Hispanic share crossed 50% in 2020 and 60% by 2025. Over the same window, the share of students who are white edged down from 8.2% to 5.0%, while small groups including students who are Asian (0.7%) and students who are multiracial (0.7%) stayed in the low single digits.

This is one of the larger demographic realignments in any New Jersey district, unfolding in the state’s biggest school system. As the Hispanic majority grows, how will the district align staffing, language services, and family outreach with a student body that looks markedly different than it did a decade ago?

No. 4 ENROLLMENT

Newark Added 11,118 Students Since 2015, a 34.6% Gain That Bucks New Jersey’s Enrollment Decline

Newark grew from 32,098 students in 2015 to 43,216 in 2026, expanding through the same pandemic years when many districts shrank.

While enrollment has slid in much of New Jersey, Newark has grown. The district enrolled 32,098 students in 2015 and 43,216 in 2026, an increase of 11,118 students, or 34.6%. Enrollment peaked at 43,980 in 2025 before easing 1.7% in 2026.

The most counterintuitive stretch came during the pandemic. Between 2019 and 2022, when many districts lost students, Newark added 4,943, a 13.9% jump. Single-year gains of 9.3% in 2021 and 3.2% in 2025 drove much of the climb, with only modest dips along the way.

Growth of this scale reshapes everything from building capacity to staffing to busing. After the first enrollment decline in years in 2026, is Newark’s long expansion leveling off, or is the 764-student drop a one-year pause?

No. 5 DEMOGRAPHICS

Newark’s Multilingual Learners More Than Tripled to 11,884, Now 27.5% of Students

The share of Newark students classified as English learners rose from 10.9% in 2015 to 27.5% in 2026.

More than one in four Newark students is now learning English. The count of students classified as limited English proficient climbed from 3,513 in 2015 to 11,884 in 2026, more than tripling, and their share of enrollment rose from 10.9% to 27.5%.

The acceleration is recent. The share held near 11-13% through 2019, then jumped to 16.8% in 2020 and kept rising, reaching 22.1% in 2023 and 28.7% in 2025 before settling at 27.5%. The growth tracks the district’s expanding Hispanic majority, which reached 62.1% in 2026. Over the same period, the share of students who are economically disadvantaged, measured by free or reduced-price lunch eligibility, stayed high, at 77.5% in 2026.

Serving nearly 12,000 multilingual learners requires bilingual teachers, translated materials, and English-language programming at scale. Can the district recruit and certify enough bilingual staff to keep pace with a multilingual population that has grown every year since 2019?

No. 6 EQUITY

In Newark’s 90% Graduating Class, Students With Disabilities Graduated at 76.5% and White Students at 96.7%

Newark’s 2025 graduation rate ranged from 96.7% for white students to 76.5% for students with disabilities, a 20-point span.

Behind Newark’s 90.0% overall four-year graduation rate in 2025 sits a wide range across student groups. Students who are white graduated at 96.7% and students who are multiracial at 95.2%, while students with disabilities graduated at 76.5%, a gap of more than 20 percentage points.

Most large subgroups clustered near the district average. Students who are economically disadvantaged graduated at 90.8%, students who are Hispanic at 90.5%, and multilingual learners at 89.7%. Students who are Black graduated at 88.5%, and students who are male at 87.1%, both below the 90.0% total. Students who are Asian, a small group of 31, graduated at 83.9%.

The lowest rate belongs to the 472 students with disabilities, the largest below-average subgroup by cohort size. As Newark’s overall rate climbs, what supports would close the gap for the students with disabilities who are graduating roughly 14 points below their peers?

No. 7 ACHIEVEMENT

Newark Students Passed NJGPA English at 65.6% but Math at 36.5% in 2025, a 29-Point Gap

On New Jersey’s high school graduation assessment, 65.6% of Newark students were proficient in English in 2025 versus 36.5% in math.

Newark students performed far better in English than in math on the 2025 NJGPA, the state’s high school proficiency assessment. Among test-takers, 65.6% were proficient or above in English language arts, compared with 36.5% in mathematics, a 29-point gap on tests given to roughly the same students.

English proficiency has held steady, at 66.1% in 2023, 67.0% in 2024, and 65.6% in 2025. Math has trailed throughout but did improve, rising from 31.4% in 2023 and 31.2% in 2024 to 36.5% in 2025, the highest of the three years. The 2025 math test counted 2,920 valid scores against 2,746 for English.

A persistent two-to-one gap between reading and math proficiency points to a subject-specific challenge rather than a general one. With math finally ticking up in 2025, is that a turning point or a single-year bump?

No. 8 COLLEGE READINESS

Newark’s AP/IB Success Rate Climbed to 16.7% as Dual Enrollment Jumped From Zero to 7.2%

The share of Newark students earning college-credit-qualifying AP/IB scores rose to 16.7% in 2024, still below the 23.0% statewide figure.

Newark’s advanced coursework is expanding on multiple fronts. In 2024, 16.7% of students scored 3 or higher on an AP exam or 4 or higher on an IB exam, up from 11.5% in 2023, though still short of the 23.0% statewide rate. Participation grew too: 28.7% of students took AP or IB coursework in 2024, up from 22.9% the year before.

The sharpest change was in dual enrollment, where students earn college credit while in high school. That figure went from 0% as recently as 2022 to 0.3% in 2023 and then 7.2% in 2024, a standing start to a meaningful share in a single year.

Newark has narrowed but not closed the gap with the state on AP/IB outcomes, even as it opened a new college-credit pathway almost overnight. Can the district keep scaling dual enrollment while pushing its AP/IB success rate toward the statewide mark?

No. 9 FINANCE

Newark Spent $21,202 Per Pupil in 2024, Up 22.6% Over a Decade and Above Most DFG A Peers

Per-pupil spending rose from $17,296 in 2014 to $21,202 in 2024, a 78th-percentile figure among peer districts whose median was $19,338.

Newark spent $21,202 per pupil in 2024, a 22.6% increase from $17,296 in 2014 and enough to place the district in the 78th percentile of its District Factor Group A peers, whose median is $19,338.

The path was not a straight climb. Per-pupil spending actually dipped to $16,336 by 2017 before rising steadily, crossing $18,000 in 2020 and $20,000 in 2023. Of every dollar spent in 2025, 53.8% went to classroom instruction, with 18.5% on support services and 17.8% on plant operations, while administration accounted for 8.0%.

Newark’s average daily enrollment reached 39,260 in 2024, the largest in the data and a major driver of total cost. With spending above the peer median and rising, how is the district balancing classroom investment against the operations and support costs of running New Jersey’s biggest school system?

No. 10 COLLEGE READINESS

Every Newark Junior Took the SAT in 2024 as Statewide Participation Sat at 62.7%

Newark has reported 100% SAT participation in five of the last six years, far above the statewide rate.

Newark gives the SAT to essentially all of its students. The district reported 100% SAT participation in 2024, against a statewide rate of 62.7%, and has hit 100% in five of the past six years: 2019, 2020, 2023, and 2024, with 98.3% in 2021 and 93.1% in 2022.

The contrast with the state has widened. Statewide participation fell from 72.2% in 2018 to 45.4% in 2021 during the pandemic and has only partly recovered, reaching 62.7% in 2024. Newark, by giving the test to every eligible student, sidestepped that decline almost entirely after lifting its own participation from 59.4% in 2018 to 100% in 2019.

Universal testing means no Newark student misses the SAT for lack of access, a meaningful equity step in a district where 77.5% of students are economically disadvantaged. As more colleges go test-optional, will universal SAT administration keep paying off for Newark’s college applicants?
